exercises.title: Cache hit vs miss — how many DB calls?
exercises.scenario: Understand which requests hit the cache and which hit the database.
exercises.expected_solution:
DB QUERY prints 3 times:
  Call 1: findById(1L) → MISS (Redis empty) → DB QUERY: city 1 → stored
  Call 2: findById(2L) → MISS                → DB QUERY: city 2 → stored
  Call 3: findById(1L) → HIT (in Redis) → no DB call
  Call 4: findById(1L) → HIT (in Redis) → no DB call
  Call 5: findById(2L) → HIT (in Redis) → no DB call
  Call 6: findById(3L) → MISS           → DB QUERY: city 3 → stored

Total DB calls: 3 (for IDs 1, 2, 3 — each queried once)
Cache hits: calls 3, 4, 5 (IDs already in Redis)

test_cases.expected:
DB QUERY prints 3 times:
  Call 1: findById(1L) → MISS (Redis empty) → DB QUERY: city 1 → stored
  Call 2: findById(2L) → MISS                → DB QUERY: city 2 → stored
  Call 3: findById(1L) → HIT (in Redis) → no DB call
  Call 4: findById(1L) → HIT (in Redis) → no DB call
  Call 5: findById(2L) → HIT (in Redis) → no DB call
  Call 6: findById(3L) → MISS           → DB QUERY: city 3 → stored

Total DB calls: 3 (for IDs 1, 2, 3 — each queried once)
Cache hits: calls 3, 4, 5 (IDs already in Redis)

hints: Cache miss: key not found in Redis → method executes → result stored
Cache hit: key found in Redis → method NOT executed
Same key (same id) after first call = always a hit (until TTL expires)

exercises.title: Complete the @Cacheable annotation
exercises.instructions: Fill in the correct annotations and attributes.

blanks.hint: Read from cache on hit, call method on miss
Always call method AND update cache
Boolean attribute to clear all entries

hints: @Cacheable: read cache first, fall through to method only on miss
@CachePut: always executes method, always updates cache
allEntries=true: clears all keys in the named cache

exercises.title: Docker Compose + application.properties for Redis
exercises.scenario: Configure Redis in Docker and connect Spring Boot.
exercises.instructions:
Write:
1. docker-compose.yml: Redis 7-alpine, port 6379, maxmemory 128mb, lru eviction
2. application.properties: Spring Data Redis connection + enable caching
3. pom.xml snippets: spring-boot-starter-data-redis + spring-boot-starter-cache
exercises.expected_solution:
# docker-compose.yml:
services:
  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    container_name: app-redis
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    command: redis-server --maxmemory 128mb --maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru
    volumes:
      - redis-data:/data

volumes:
  redis-data:

# application.properties:
spring.data.redis.host=localhost
spring.data.redis.port=6379
spring.cache.type=redis
spring.cache.redis.time-to-live=300000
logging.level.org.springframework.cache=TRACE

<!-- pom.xml:
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-redis</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
  <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-cache</artifactId>
</dependency>
-->
hints: maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru: evict least-recently-used keys when memory full
spring.cache.type=redis: tells Spring Boot to use Redis as cache provider
logging.level.org.springframework.cache=TRACE: shows cache hits/misses in logs

exercises.title: Add @Cacheable to a service method
exercises.scenario: Most basic caching pattern: @Cacheable on a read method.
exercises.instructions:
Add @Cacheable to Zerodha's InstrumentService:
1. @EnableCaching on @SpringBootApplication
2. findBySymbol(symbol): cache in 'instruments', key=symbol
3. findAll(): cache in 'instruments', key='all'
4. getPrice(symbol): separate 'prices' cache, key=symbol
5. updatePrice(symbol, price): @CacheEvict from 'prices'

All methods compile correctly.
hints: @EnableCaching on @SpringBootApplication: required to activate proxy
SpEL string literal: key="'all'" (single quotes inside double quotes)
@CacheEvict: evicts BEFORE method runs by default (beforeInvocation=false = evict after)

exercises.title: Custom RedisCacheManager with per-cache TTL
exercises.scenario: Different caches need different TTLs — configure them programmatically.
exercises.instructions:
Write a @Configuration class FlipkartCacheConfig:
1. 'products' cache: 10-minute TTL
2. 'prices' cache: 30-second TTL
3. 'categories' cache: 1-hour TTL
4. Default: 5-minute TTL
5. All caches use JSON serialization (GenericJackson2JsonRedisSerializer)
6. Null values not cached
hints: RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig(): starting point for customization
entryTtl(Duration): sets TTL per cache config
withInitialCacheConfigurations: maps cache name → config with specific TTL

exercises.title: @Caching — multiple evictions on one method
exercises.scenario: @Caching groups multiple cache operations on one method.
exercises.instructions:
Write ProductService.deleteProduct(id) that:
1. Deletes from DB
2. Evicts from 'products' cache (key = id)
3. Evicts ALL entries from 'categories' cache
4. Evicts ALL entries from 'search' cache
5. Use @Caching to group the three @CacheEvict operations
hints: @Caching(evict={...}): groups multiple @CacheEvict in one annotation
Java doesn't allow duplicate annotations — @Caching is the workaround
allEntries=true: removes all keys with this cache name prefix

exercises.title: Integration test: verify cache with @SpringBootTest
exercises.scenario: Test that caching actually works — method called only once for two requests.
exercises.instructions:
Write an @SpringBootTest integration test:
1. Call cityService.findById(1L) twice
2. Assert: the underlying repo method was called only ONCE (Mockito spy)
3. Assert: second call returns same object as first
4. Call @CacheEvict then call again — assert: repo called once more
hints: @SpyBean: real Spring bean wrapped with Mockito spy for call verification
cacheManager.getCache(name).clear(): programmatically clear a cache in tests
verify(repo, times(1)): assert repository called exactly once

exercises.title: Conditional caching with unless and condition
exercises.scenario: Cache only when result meets a condition; skip cache for certain inputs.
exercises.instructions:
Write a ProductService with conditional caching:
1. findByCategory(category): cache UNLESS result is empty
2. findById(id): cache ONLY if id doesn't start with 'TEST-'
3. searchByName(query): cache ONLY if query.length >= 3 (no caching for single chars)
4. getVipPrice(productId, userId): don't cache VIP prices (user-specific)

Use 'unless' and 'condition' attributes correctly.
hints: condition: SpEL evaluated BEFORE method call — use for input-based decisions
unless: SpEL evaluated AFTER method call — use for result-based decisions
#result: refers to the method's return value in 'unless' expression

exercises.title: Programmatic cache access with CacheManager
exercises.scenario: Sometimes you need to interact with the cache directly, not via annotations.
exercises.instructions:
Write a CacheWarmupService that:
1. Injects CacheManager
2. @PostConstruct: pre-loads top 5 cities into 'cities' cache
3. manualEvict(String cacheName, Object key): evicts a specific key
4. getCacheSize(String cacheName): returns count of keys (using RedisTemplate)
5. Show: why pre-warming is useful for cold-start performance
hints: cacheManager.getCache(name): get Cache object for programmatic access
cache.put(key, value): manually store a value without @Cacheable
redisTemplate.keys(pattern): find Redis keys matching a pattern

exercises.title: Self-invocation bypass — why the cache doesn't work
exercises.scenario: Calling @Cacheable from within the same class bypasses the Spring AOP proxy.
exercises.expected_solution:
'DB HIT' prints 3 times (for O1, O2, O1)

Reason: self-invocation bypasses the Spring AOP proxy.
Spring Cache works by wrapping the bean in a proxy:
  Proxy intercepts external calls → checks cache before calling real method.

When findMultiple() calls this.findById(), it calls the REAL object
(not the proxy). The cache proxy is NEVER involved.
Result: every findById() call inside findMultiple() always hits DB.

Fix options:
1. Inject self-reference (cleanest for simple cases):
   @Autowired OrderService self;  // Spring injects the proxy
   return ids.stream().map(id -> self.findById(id)).toList();

2. Move @Cacheable to a separate CacheableOrderRepository class:
   @Autowired CacheableOrderRepository cachedRepo;
   return ids.stream().map(id -> cachedRepo.findById(id)).toList();

3. Use ApplicationContext.getBean() to get the proxied instance.

Best practice: keep @Cacheable methods in a separate service layer
that other beans inject — never call them internally from the same class.

test_cases.expected:
'DB HIT' prints 3 times (for O1, O2, O1)

Reason: self-invocation bypasses the Spring AOP proxy.
Spring Cache works by wrapping the bean in a proxy:
  Proxy intercepts external calls → checks cache before calling real method.

When findMultiple() calls this.findById(), it calls the REAL object
(not the proxy). The cache proxy is NEVER involved.
Result: every findById() call inside findMultiple() always hits DB.

Fix options:
1. Inject self-reference (cleanest for simple cases):
   @Autowired OrderService self;  // Spring injects the proxy
   return ids.stream().map(id -> self.findById(id)).toList();

2. Move @Cacheable to a separate CacheableOrderRepository class:
   @Autowired CacheableOrderRepository cachedRepo;
   return ids.stream().map(id -> cachedRepo.findById(id)).toList();

3. Use ApplicationContext.getBean() to get the proxied instance.

Best practice: keep @Cacheable methods in a separate service layer
that other beans inject — never call them internally from the same class.

hints: Spring AOP creates a proxy around the bean — only external calls go through the proxy
this.findById() bypasses the proxy entirely → no cache check
Fix: inject the bean as a dependency (self-reference) to go through the proxy

exercises.title: Composite cache key with SpEL
exercises.scenario: Cache keys often need multiple parameters combined.
exercises.instructions:
Write a ProductSearchService with composite keys:
1. search(city, category, maxPrice): cache key = city + ':' + category + ':' + maxPrice
2. getTopRated(category, limit): key = category + ':top:' + limit
3. getDeals(city, date): key = city + ':deals:' + date.toString()
4. getUserRecommendations(userId, category): key using T(java.util.Objects).hash(userId, category)
5. Test: verify different parameter combinations produce different keys
hints: SpEL string concatenation: #city + ':' + #category (+ operator in SpEL)
Method calls in SpEL: #date.toString() — works for any Java method
T() operator: calls static methods, e.g. T(java.util.Objects).hash(...)

exercises.title: Complete Swiggy restaurant caching layer
exercises.scenario: Multi-level caching with eviction, conditional logic, and pre-warming.
exercises.instructions:
Build complete Swiggy restaurant caching:
1. CacheConfig: 'restaurants'=5min, 'cuisines'=1hr, 'popular'=2min TTLs, JSON serializer
2. RestaurantService:
   - getByCity(city): @Cacheable unless empty
   - getByCuisine(cuisine): @Cacheable  
   - getPopular(city): @Cacheable 'popular' cache
   - update(restaurant): @Caching evict from both 'restaurants' and 'popular'
3. CacheWarmup: @PostConstruct pre-loads top 3 cities
4. @SpringBootTest integration test: verify second call doesn't hit repository
exercises.starter_code:
// Complete Swiggy restaurant caching
// CacheConfig, RestaurantService, CacheWarmup, @SpringBootTest test
hints: unless on @Cacheable: skip caching when city has no restaurants
@Caching evicts both restaurants and popular for the same city
@PostConstruct warmup calls through the Spring proxy (external call) — cache works

exercises.title: RedisTemplate for direct Redis operations
exercises.scenario: Sometimes you need direct Redis access beyond @Cacheable for custom data structures.
exercises.instructions:
Write a SessionService using RedisTemplate directly:
1. saveSession(userId, sessionData): store with 30-minute expiry
2. getSession(userId): retrieve session, return null if expired
3. extendSession(userId, extraMinutes): refresh the TTL
4. invalidateSession(userId): delete the key
5. Show the Redis key pattern and data format
hints: redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(key, value, duration): SET key value EX seconds
redisTemplate.expire(key, duration): EXPIRE key seconds — refreshes TTL
redisTemplate.hasKey(key): EXISTS key — returns Boolean

exercises.title: Cache statistics monitoring via Actuator
exercises.scenario: Monitor cache hit rate and size to tune TTL and spot cache misses.
exercises.instructions:
Configure and use Spring Cache statistics:
1. Enable cache statistics in application.properties
2. @SpringBootApplication with metrics enabled
3. CacheMonitorService: reads cache stats via MeterRegistry
4. @Scheduled every minute: logs hit rate for each cache
5. Alert if hit rate drops below 80% for 'products' cache

All code compiles.
hints: spring.cache.redis.enable-statistics=true: activates Micrometer cache metrics
cache.gets with tag result='hit'/'miss': the key metrics for hit rate calculation
@Scheduled requires @EnableScheduling on @SpringBootApplication

exercises.title: Complete Zerodha instrument cache — synthesis
exercises.scenario: Full production caching: config, service, warmup, eviction, monitoring.
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete Zerodha instrument data caching system:
1. CacheConfig: 'instruments'=6hr, 'quotes'=5s, 'indices'=30s TTLs + JSON
2. InstrumentService: @Cacheable findBySymbol, findAll (key='all'), @CacheEvict on update
3. QuoteService: @Cacheable getLatestQuote(symbol, exchange)
4. CacheWarmupService: @PostConstruct pre-loads all instruments (findAll)
5. CacheMonitorService: @Scheduled logs hit rates every 5 minutes
6. @SpringBootTest: integration test verifying cache behavior

All code compiles and test passes.
hints: quotes TTL=5s: stock prices change every tick; short TTL prevents stale data
instruments TTL=6hr: company data changes rarely; long TTL reduces DB load
Pre-warm findAll(): populates 'instruments::all' cache before market opens
